question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: realm] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that realm=0 is the default and no validation enforces that realm matches the actual network's realm, by submitting fee estimation requests that assume realm=0 on a non-zero-realm network, causing the fee context to compute fees for the wrong realm and return incorrect fee amounts?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: shard] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shard=0 default with no upper-bound validation to submit crafted EVM address parameters (e.g., '1.2.0xABCD...') that override the shard/realm from CommonProperties, causing fee collector resolution to use a different shard than the configured one and misroute fee payments?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Class: DatabaseStartupProperties] Can an unprivileged external user set all DatabaseStartupProperties timeouts to their minimum values simultaneously (connectTimeout=1s, socketTimeout=1s, validationTimeout=1s, interval=500ms, timeout=10s), causing the database waiter to exhaust all retries in 10 seconds with 500ms intervals, preventing fee schedule data from being loaded and causing the node to serve empty fee schedules?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: validationTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user set DatabaseStartupProperties.validationTimeout to a value that causes Math.toIntExact() in DatabaseWaiter to throw ArithmeticException (overflow from long to int), crashing the startup sequence and leaving fee schedule tables in an uninitialized state?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: realm] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that CommonProperties.realm is used in NetworkProperties.createUnreleasedSupplyRangeBounds() to compute EntityId bounds for unreleased supply accounts, by injecting realm=Long.MAX_VALUE to cause EntityId.of() to produce IDs that overlap with fee collector accounts, corrupting the supply calculation and indirectly affecting fee-related financial invariants?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: init()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a Spring Boot prototype-scoped misconfiguration that creates multiple CommonProperties instances, causing init() to be called multiple times and INSTANCE to be overwritten with a new object mid-flight, so that concurrent fee estimation threads read different realm/shard values and produce inconsistent fee amounts for the same transaction type?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: INSTANCE] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the AtomicReference<CommonProperties> INSTANCE by triggering a Spring context close-and-restart cycle (e.g., via a public health endpoint that triggers a restart) to set INSTANCE to a new CommonProperties with default realm=0/shard=0, causing all fee estimations after restart to use wrong network parameters until the next @PostConstruct completes?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: connectTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user inject connectTimeout=2147483647 (Integer.MAX_VALUE seconds) via environment variable, causing the JDBC connectTimeout property passed in DatabaseWaiter to be set to an astronomically large value, effectively disabling connection timeouts and allowing an attacker-controlled database proxy to hold connections open indefinitely while serving manipulated fee schedule data?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: socketTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user set socketTimeout to a very large value (no @DurationMax constraint present), causing JDBC socket reads in DatabaseWaiter to never time out, allowing a man-in-the-middle attacker to inject arbitrary fee schedule data into the database connection stream without detection?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Class: CommonProperties] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @ConfigurationProperties('hiero.mirror.common') binding to inject malformed Duration strings (e.g., 'PT-1S') for connectTimeout that pass Spring's converter but produce negative Duration objects, bypassing @DurationMin(seconds=1) and causing JDBC to use a negative timeout value that is interpreted as infinite, enabling fee schedule data poisoning via slow DB responses?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: realm] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of cross-field validation between realm and shard in CommonProperties to supply a realm/shard combination that is valid individually (@Min(0)) but invalid for the target network, causing FeeEstimationFeeContext to initialize with a CONFIG_PROVIDER that maps to a non-existent network configuration and returns default (zero) fees for all transaction types?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getInstance()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the static nature of getInstance() — which reads from a JVM-global AtomicReference — to cause a class-loader isolation attack where a fee estimation component loaded in a child class loader sees a different INSTANCE than the main application, resulting in fee calculations using a different realm/shard than the rest of the system?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: timeout] Can an unprivileged external user set DatabaseStartupProperties.timeout to exactly 10 seconds (the @DurationMin minimum), then cause repeated transient database failures lasting >10 seconds, forcing the application to throw IllegalStateException from DatabaseWaiter and restart with an empty fee schedule cache, causing all fee estimations to return zero until the next successful startup?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: realm] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that realm is used in FeeEstimationFeeContext's static CONFIG_PROVIDER (initialized once at class load) to cause a permanent fee miscalculation by ensuring the class is loaded during a window when realm has a wrong value, since the static field is never refreshed even if CommonProperties is later updated?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: shard] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the static initialization of FeeEstimationFeeContext.CONFIG_PROVIDER which calls CommonProperties.getInstance().getShard() at class-load time, by triggering class load before @PostConstruct init() runs, causing getInstance() to throw IllegalStateException that propagates as an ExceptionInInitializerError, permanently breaking fee estimation for the JVM lifetime?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Class: DatabaseStartupProperties] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a @DurationMax constraint on DatabaseStartupProperties.timeout to set timeout=Duration.ofDays(Long.MAX_VALUE), causing System.nanoTime() + properties.getTimeout().toNanos() in DatabaseWaiter to overflow to a negative deadline, making the while loop condition always false and skipping all database connectivity checks, allowing the application to start with no fee schedule data loaded?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: realm] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @Data-generated setRealm() method (no access control) combined with a Spring Boot Actuator /configprops endpoint to discover the current realm value and then use /env POST to change it, causing all subsequent fee collector entity ID resolutions to use the attacker-chosen realm and redirect fee payments?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: connectTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the String.valueOf(properties.getConnectTimeout().toSeconds()) conversion in DatabaseWaiter to inject a connectTimeout Duration whose toSeconds() returns 0 (e.g., Duration.ofMillis(999)), bypassing the @DurationMin(seconds=1) annotation if the validator is not triggered for programmatic sets, causing JDBC to use a zero connect timeout and enabling connection-timing attacks against fee schedule ingestion?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: interval] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Uninterruptibles.sleepUninterruptibly(properties.getInterval()) call in DatabaseWaiter by setting interval to a very large Duration (no @DurationMax), causing the retry loop to sleep for an arbitrarily long time between attempts, effectively preventing fee schedule data from being loaded within any reasonable timeframe?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: realm] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that realm=0 is used as the default in EntityIdEvmAddressParameter.valueOfNullable() when no realm is specified in the EVM address string, by submitting fee-related API requests with bare EVM addresses that resolve to realm=0 entities even on a non-zero-realm network, causing fee collector lookups to fail silently and fees to be assessed against null collectors?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getInstance()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getInstance() throws IllegalStateException (not a checked exception) when INSTANCE is null, causing fee estimation callers that catch Exception broadly to swallow the error and return a default fee of zero, allowing transactions to be submitted with no fees charged?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonProperties.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Field: shard] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shard field's lack of network-consistency validation — CommonProperties does not verify that shard matches the actual Hedera network's shard — to configure a mirror node with shard=1 pointing at a shard=0 network, causing all fee schedule lookups to use wrong entity IDs and return incorrect fee amounts to API consumers?
